data.title: Coherent: A Compelling Opportunity For Patient Investors
data.description:
I have written about several optical/laser companies over the last several months, including Lumentum Holdings (LITE) at the end of December and Ciena (CIEN) on February 1. This article will be my first look at Coherent (COHR). Although Coherent has done well over the last year, up 152.9%, it has not done nearly as well as its most similar competitor, Lumentum, which is up 571.9%.

Lumentum's emphasis on optical equipment sales to Artificial Intelligence ("AI") and cloud companies has driven accelerating revenue growth. It has also handily beaten analysts' quarterly expectations over the last year.

On the other hand, although Coherent has deep exposure to the Artificial Intelligence ("AI") optical market, it is less focused than Lumentum. Coherent also manufactures lasers for industrial manufacturing (cutting, welding, and engraving), medical applications (LASIK eye surgery and similar dermatology lasers for skin conditions), aerospace (3D mapping via LiDAR and optical space communication), and semiconductor manufacturing equipment. The company also develops 3D sensing components for smartphones and advanced optical sensors. Coherent is a more extensive, more diversified "generalist" laser and photonics company with more growth drivers than AI alone can provide.

Coherent's diversification has several advantages: it is slightly less exposed to potential cyclicalities arising from inventory corrections or hyperscaler AI spending cycles. Additionally, it has significant exposure to several markets that may also eventually experience outsize growth, including Silicon Carbide (SiC) power electronics (used in Electric Vehicles), automotive LiDAR, Augmented Reality ("AR")/Virtual Reality ("VR") displays, industrial laser automation, quantum optical technologies, and advanced sensing.

However, AI optics is currently booming, and Coherent's slightly lower exposure to AI optics than Lumentum shows up in the financial results. Lumentum's second quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 year-over-year revenue growth was 65%, compared to Coherent's 17% year-over-year growth.

AI optics also has higher margins, and Lumentum's greater exposure to AI optics may be reflected in its higher gross margins. Coherent's second quarter non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) gross margin was 39%, 3.5 points lower than Lumentum's second quarter non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) gross margin of 42.5%.

Additionally, although Coherent has beaten analysts' revenue and earnings estimates over the last year, bullish investors haven't always been satisfied with management's forward guidance. For example, in the fourth-quarter 2025 results, management provided first-quarter FY 2026 revenue guidance with a midpoint of $1.53 billion, less than analysts' estimates of $1.55 billion. Although the market was fully aware that the revenue guidance miss resulted from the divestiture of its Aerospace and Defense business unit, the stock declined 19.61% on August 14, 2025, the day after the fourth-quarter earnings release. The market may have been underwhelmed by the guidance relative to Lumentum's.

Even when Coherent beat analyst revenue, earnings, and guidance, investors haven't always been satisfied. For instance, the stock declined by 21% from the February 4 closing price of $227.68 to the February 5 market opening price of $179.11. The stock rose throughout the day as investors refocused on its long-term upside potential, and the stock closed approximately 1% below the February 4 close. The company's valuation is extraordinarily sensitive to its monetary performance.

Although Lumentum's recent financial track record relative to Coherent makes it appear a better bet, there are reasons to consider investing in Coherent either alongside Lumentum or instead of Lumentum. Coherent has several advantages that the market expects to begin to pay off in 2027.

Analysts expect FY 2027 revenue to increase to 24.17% from 19.06% in FY 2026. One reason for analysts' confidence in a strong Coherent FY 2027 is rising demand for its data center products and the expectation for accelerating revenue growth over the next several quarters. Chief Executive ("CEO") James Anderson said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

As a result of the AI build-out, we saw strong revenue and profit growth in our December quarter. We also experienced another step function increase in our bookings, which we expect to increase again in our current quarter. Given the extraordinary strength and visibility of demand from our customers, combined with our continued rapid expansion and production capacity, we expect a period of sustained strong revenue growth over the coming quarters. In particular, we expect continued strong sequential revenue growth in both our March and June quarters, and we expect our fiscal '27 revenue growth rate to exceed our fiscal '26 growth rate.

Although Coherent has less exposure to AI than Lumentum, it still benefits greatly from AI data center growth. Much of the company's revenue growth is powered by sales of lasers for its 800 G and 1.6 T transceivers and Optical Circuit Switch ("OCS"). CEO Anderson added the following on the earnings call, pointing out the company's competitive advantages:

Data center's...book-to-bill ratio...exceeded 4x as customer demand continues to increase and customers place orders further out in time, which provides us with strong visibility for the coming quarters. The strength of our product portfolio, combined with our vertical integration and our expanding U.S. manufacturing footprint, provide a clear competitive advantage with our customers.

Vertical integration means that Coherent controls its product lifecycle from raw materials through finished subsystems, conceptually enabling it to produce laser chips at lower cost over time. Theoretically, Coherent should achieve higher gross margins than its competitors. The company also exhibits operating leverage, meaning operating income should grow faster than revenue. An example is the company's second-quarter GAAP operating income, which grew 34.40%, faster than revenue, which grew 17.48%. Investors should monitor future quarters to see if the company can maintain operating leverage.

Although Lumentum's stock has recently outperformed Coherent's, this may not always be the case. If Coherent's structural advantages translate into financial advantages relative to competitors, it could outperform the Laser Systems, Components, and Materials industry, including Lumentum, in the future.

Let us examine its primary method for controlling manufacturing costs: increasing the size of the laser chip wafer.

Increasing Wafer Size From 3-inch to 6-inch

If you are unfamiliar with lasers, you might assume, as I did, that lasers used for fiber optics are similar to traditional gas-tube lasers used for cutting and engraving. Industrial lasers work by creating a high-voltage electrical discharge in a gaseous mixture, such as CO₂ or Helium-Neon, which produces photons. Using mirrors, these photons are focused into a laser beam.

While industrial lasers use gases to generate photons, fiber-optic lasers use a solid-state semiconductor made of a material such as Indium Phosphide (InP) to generate photons. When electricity is applied to a specific region of a semiconductor material called the active region, it releases energy in the form of a photon.

InP emits infrared photons, the ideal wavelength range for propagation through fiber-optic glass with low signal loss. As a result, you will see InP primarily used in high-performance optical lasers for long-haul telecommunications, data center interconnects, and sensing technologies, namely LIDAR. Semiconductor lasers are also thousands of times smaller, faster, and more efficient than industrial lasers. Photonics Manufacturers can also produce many more lasers operating at the microscopic scale than those manufacturers producing gas-tube lasers for large-scale uses.

Coherent's vertical integration advantages are evident in InP lasers. Laser manufacturers that lack vertical integration purchase InP wafers from other companies, such as Sumitomo Electric, AXT, Inc., and InPACT.

In contrast, Coherent buys high‑purity Indium and other elemental precursors to grow its own InP epitaxial structures and converts them into wafer substrates. By controlling the InP wafer manufacturing process, the company avoids the extra fees third-party InP wafer producers add to the final product, improves the number of chips a wafer can produce, and reduces material costs, factors that can directly support higher gross margins relative to competitors who rely on third-party suppliers of finished InP wafers.

We will now discuss how Coherent is improving the number of usable chips per wafer relative to the total number of possible chips on a wafer, a metric referred to as yield. On the company's most recent earnings call, CEO Anderson discussed the benefits of increasing the size of the InP wafer from three inches to six inches: "As a reminder, our indium phosphide capacity expansion is driven by a ramp of 6-inch wafer production; a 6-inch wafer compared to a 3-inch wafer will produce more than 4x as many chips at less than half the cost."

Making wafers larger brings several benefits, including more usable chips per wafer because of the way chips fit on a round surface. Since chips are rectangular and wafers are round, some space at the edges is always wasted. To keep it simple, Coherent can fit many more usable chips on a 6-inch wafer than on a 3-inch one. Also, since labor, electricity, and cleanroom costs are the same for both sizes, making more chips on a larger wafer lowers the cost per chip. The company can also more easily automate the production of 6-inch wafers, further reducing costs. According to a 2024 Coherent blog, there is a "60% reduction in die costs for devices widely used in a broad range of applications." A die is the material cut from a wafer that contains a functional integrated circuit.

The benefits of vertical integration and increasing wafer size are already evident in improved margins. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Sherri Luther said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

Our Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 39%, a 24 basis point improvement compared to the prior quarter and a 77 basis point improvement as compared to the year ago quarter. We continue to execute on our gross margin expansion strategy, where we generated sequential and year-over-year increases in gross margin, primarily in the Datacenter & Communications segment. These improvements were driven by reductions in product input costs, efficiency gains from improved cycle times in the manufacturing process as well as yield improvements. Pricing optimization also continued to contribute meaningfully to our gross margin expansion.

Lastly, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch should increase Coherent's production capacity, which should help boost revenue growth over the next few years. CEO Anderson said, "We expect both fiscal '26 and fiscal '27 to be strong growth years for Coherent given our exceptional demand from our customers and the rapid expansion of our production capacity."

Coherent's capacity for increased revenue growth and its possible cost advantage over competing laser manufacturers may show up in the stock's outperformance over the next few years.

Coherent's Financial Condition

Coherent beat second quarter fiscal year ("FY") analysts' revenue estimates by 2.74% and earnings-per-share ("EPS") estimates by 6.6%.

Coherent closed the sale of its Munich-based Rofin brand to Bystronic in late January. The Rofin Brand specializes in industrial gas-tube CO2 lasers, although it also manufactures solid-state and fiber lasers. Coherent sold the brand to refocus the business more toward lasers for telecommunications and AI data centers and away from manufacturing finished industrial lasers, which Rofin Brand focuses on. Coherent will use the proceeds of the sale to repay debt, giving it financial agility and helping it to increase investment in InP solid-state lasers. In the short term, annual revenue will decline by approximately $100 million, or $25 million per quarter, but the company's margins and growth profile should improve. CEO Anderson said on the earnings call, "The sale of this [Munich] product division is expected to be immediately accretive to both gross margin and EPS."

Management's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue guidance midpoint of $1.77 billion topped analysts' third-quarter revenue estimates of $1.70 billion. Following the company's second-quarter earnings release, analysts raised their estimates to match management's guidance of $1.77 billion.

Management's third-quarter FY 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance midpoint was $1.38, which beat the analysts' consensus estimate of $1.32. Following the company's second-quarter earnings release, analysts raised their EPS estimates to $1.39, which was $0.01 above management's guidance.

Although the company's revenue and EPS outperformed, some investors are dissatisfied with Coherent's operating margin expectations, which are lower than Lumentum's. Coherent doesn't explicitly forecast operating margin. Still, investors can use revenue estimates, non-GAAP gross margins, and non-GAAP operating expenses ("OpEx") to calculate Coherent's implied non-GAAP operating margin of 20.9% at the midpoint of guidance.

In contrast, Lumentum predicts its third quarter FY 2026 non-GAAP operating margin to rise from 25.2% in the second quarter to 30.5% at the midpoint of guidance. Management gave an optimistic margin forecast for three reasons: first, the company has operating leverage (meaning operating profits will grow faster than revenue), and management forecast revenue growth of 89% at the midpoint of guidance. Second, they expect a favorable shift toward a mix of higher-margin products. Third, the company expects better manufacturing operational efficiency through improving chip yields and better capacity utilization of its fabs.

Lumentum's ~30% non-GAAP operating margin forecast implies that the company may be moving beyond the heavy AI investment phase and toward the harvesting phase. In contrast, Coherent is still in the middle of a massive Capital Expenditure ("CapEx") cycle to fund expanding production capacity by building 6-inch wafers.

Although CapEx (balance sheet) and OpEx (income statement) are in different accounting buckets, when a company increases CapEx, OpEx eventually also increases as a delayed effect for several reasons. The most direct reason is that when a company invests in new photolithography equipment and laser etching machines, it must eventually depreciate them over their useful lives (usually five to seven years). Depreciation, a non-cash expense, is sometimes recorded on the income statement as Cost of Goods Sold and sometimes as OpEx, both of which reduce the operating margin.

Additionally, increasing CapEx can cause numerous indirect operating expenses. For instance, new 6-inch wafer fabs require hiring engineers to operate the expensive equipment, paying for utilities (electricity and water), acquiring spare parts, performing maintenance, and absorbing costs associated with defective chips (yield loss). All of these costs are recorded as OpEx. As OpEx rises, operating margins decline.

When investors compare these two companies today, they see one company whose investments are paying off today (Lumentum) and another where it may take several more years for the investments to pay off (Coherent). Generally, the market assigns a higher value to a company that yields a higher return today than to one forecast to yield a higher return down the road.

Earlier in this article, I stated, "The company's valuation is extraordinarily sensitive to its monetary performance." If the market has priced in a financial showing that Coherent fails to meet, despite "beating analysts' expectations," investors may re-rate the valuation lower, which was a major reason for the initial 19% drop on the February 5 open that I mentioned earlier in this article.

The company's cash and restricted cash are $899.19 million. Its long-term debt is 3.2454 billion (From its balance sheet). The long-term debt listed on YCharts is slightly off because sometimes third-party data providers include other reported figures in their definition of long-term debt. I included YCharts' long-term debt data because it has historically been directionally accurate.

The company's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.4, indicating that Coherent conservatively finances its operations with more equity than debt.

If you look up this company's net debt-to-EBITDA (Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) on different third-party websites, you will see different figures. Many third-party websites report Coherent's net debt-to-EBITDA ratio as 2.8. However, a calculated net debt-to-EBITDA ratio based on Seeking Alpha's figures (as of February 10) is 2.21, using net debt of $2,675.8 million and TTM EBITDA of $1,208.2 million. There are several reasons why different websites may report different figures.

Disparate financial websites may define net debt differently. For instance, some may use the standard definition of total debt (short-term plus long-term) minus cash and cash equivalents. Others may use adjusted net debt, which may include lease liabilities, restricted cash adjustments, pension obligations, exclude cash held for M&A/escrow, exclude short‑term investments, or treat marketable securities differently. Similarly, different websites calculate EBITDA differently.

Generally, when I analyze a company, I use the debt-to-EBITDA figures it provides because the company likely uses lenders' covenant definitions for net debt and EBITDA, meaning the company's earnings report refers to figures lenders use to assess its debt. CFO Luther stated on the earnings call: "We maintained our debt leverage ratio at 1.7x, down from 2.3x in the year-ago quarter," which shows the company is deleveraging. Coherent either reduced its debt, increased its EBITDA, or did both. At a debt leverage ratio of 1.7x, it has room to fund Capital Expenditure ("CapEx"), ramp production, or pursue small acquisitions without instant pressure to raise equity or reduce investment.

The company's Datacenter & Communications segment, which now accounts for over 70% of its revenue, generates most of Coherent's revenue growth. Most of that segment's growth comes from 800G optical transceivers, and the company has just begun production of next-generation 1.6T optical transceivers. The tremendous demand from data centers and communication providers for 800G and the new 1.6T optical transceivers has created an InP supply-demand imbalance, straining the whole laser industry's ability to create enough InP wafers to keep up with demand. CEO Anderson addressed the InP supply-demand situation on the second-quarter earnings call:

It seems like every quarter, we think we're going to catch up and then the [InP] demand keeps increasing. So I don't foresee the supply/demand getting back in balance this calendar year. I don't think it happens next calendar year. And if the forecast that we're seeing from customers for indium phosphide laser supply that they need for scale up, I think we could be in a very sustained long period of supply-demand imbalance on indium phosphide, which is exactly why we're super focused on ramping our 6-inch capacity as quickly as possible. That near-term goal is to double our capacity by the end of this year, but we're driving goals beyond that, that are very aggressive in terms of our continued ramp of indium phosphide capacity. And the demand that we're seeing from the customers absorbs all of that capacity and then some.

When Coherent accelerates efforts to "double" InP wafer capacity by the end of 2026, it will require fab expansion, procurement of precursor InP materials, and the creation of an inventory buildup of InP wafers, all of which will consume substantial cash. Many things can suppress Cash From Operations ("CFO"). Still, it is likely that a significant portion of Coherent's second-quarter FY 2026 69% decline in cash from operations reflects efforts to increase InP supply.

As a result of the second quarter of FY 2026, cash from operations declined to $57.90 million, and CapEx increased to $153.60 million. The company incurred $95.70 million in Free Cash Flow ("FCF") losses in the second quarter, which is something some investors will balk at.

Even if the company raises non-GAAP operating margins to the high 20s or low 30s, investors may not increase the stock's valuation until it returns to FCF-positive status.

Valuation

Coherent's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) is 6.230, significantly above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians. The market either overvalues the stock or expects accelerating revenue growth, propelled by its brightening prospects in AI optics. However, if the company fails to achieve the expected revenue growth, the stock could drop. I am not sure whether its valuation will drop to its three-year median, as that was the level before AI began to have a significant impact on revenue. However, if the company misses revenue targets, the stock will likely have a significant selloff. Coherent's three-year median corresponds to a stock price of $75.34, a 67% decline from its February 10 closing price of $228.37.

The following table compares Coherent's two-year forward P/S ratio with its estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) and TTM profitability, as well as those of several peers in the optical components and lasers industry. Generally, investors consider stocks with the highest P/S or P/S/G ratio to be overvalued.

Company Name FY 2027 forward P/S ratio FY 2027 estimated forward revenue growth FY 2027 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Coherent 4.97 24.17% 0.21 -1.6% 36.2% 8.1% 4%
Lumentum Holdings 10.51 55.52% 0.19 -1.4% 33.4% 1.2% 4.1%
IPG Photonics (IPGP) 4.24 8.04% 0.53 3.8% 38.7% 2.5% 0.9%
Viavi Solutions (VIAV) 3.77 10.64% 0.35 6.3% 56.6% 3.4% 4.5%
Fabrinet (FN) 3.59 15.31% 0.23 2.6% 12% 9.7% 48.1%
Marvell (MRVL) 6.89 22.19% 0.31 20.3% 50.7% 14.8% 5.9%
Ciena (CIEN) 5.65 16.41% 0.34 13.9% 42% 4.1% 4.4%
Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) 3.96 68.40% 0.06 -71.6% 29.3% -11.8% -10.4%
Median 4.24 16.41% 0.31 3.8% 38.7% 3.4% 4.4%

Source: Forward revenue growth rates come from Seeking Alpha. TTM profitability comes from a third-party website.

On the one hand, some may consider Coherent's two-year forward P/S ratio of 4.97 to be overvalued relative to the peer median of 4.24. On the other hand, the peer group's median P/S/G is 0.31, higher than Coherent's P/S/G of 0.21, which some investors may consider a sign of undervaluation based solely on P/S/G.

Things become more muddled when business profitability measures are included in the equation. First, be aware that I am using trailing 12-month ("TTM") GAAP profitability and not quarterly numbers in the comparison. TTM metrics may not completely capture the company's performance in its recent quarter. Additionally, elsewhere in this article, I discuss Coherent and Lumentum's non-GAAP operating margins. So, be careful not to directly compare GAAP to non-GAAP or TTM to forward-looking profitability measures.

Coherent's FCF and gross margins are below the medians. Its operating margin exceeds the median of its peers. The company's ROIC is below the median. Its profitability measures indicate a mixed picture.

Given that future profitability could improve from higher yields on 6‑inch wafers, new capacity coming online, and vertical integration, the stock appears fairly valued, with potential upside if the company's strategies result in it outperforming analysts' revenue and earnings-per-share ("EPS") estimates in future quarters.

The company's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 181.45, well above the Information Technology sector median of 31.26. Investors may interpret this as indicating that the market overvalues the stock or has already priced in accelerating EPS growth.

Coherent's FY 2026 forward P/E to analysts' estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.82, calculated by dividing its forward P/E of 42.60 by its estimated EPS growth of 51.85%. Generally, a PEG ratio below 1.0 is considered indicative of undervaluation.

Suppose the stock sells at its FY 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0 (a sign of fair value); the stock price would be $277.91, a 22% increase over its February 10 closing price.

Generally, the market will allow a growth stock's PEG ratio to reach 2.0. However, PEG ratios of 2.0 and higher are generally reserved for software or cloud growth companies.

Coherent is a hardware manufacturer. Even with the tailwinds of AI, the market rarely, if ever, values a fiber optics or networking company at a forward PEG ratio of 2.0. The maximum forward PEG ratio that the market might be willing to assign to a company like Coherent is 1.50, considering that the market currently values Ciena at a FY 2027 forward PEG ratio of 1.52 (Ciena forward P/E of 44.13 divided by its EPS growth rate of 28.95)

If Coherent reaches a 2026 forward PEG ratio of 1.50, the stock price would be $416.87, an 82.5% rise above its February 10 closing price. If it sold at its FY 2027 PEG ratio of 1.5, its price would be $388.43, which is 70% above its February 10 closing price.

YChart shows that the company's TTM price-to-FCF (P/FCF) is 418.65, which I had to verify since the company currently has a TTM FCF of negative $103.62 million, and negative FCF can't produce a positive P/FCF. One explanation is that YChart's figure may use a different TTM window or FCF definition. Coherent's P/FCF is either negative or barely positive; therefore, the P/FCF valuation metric is unreliable for this stock.

Some bullish investors will view these numbers through the lens of short-term pain for long-term gain. They view a negative or extremely high P/FCF ratio as a short-term issue to be tolerated to realize anticipated long-term gains in 2027, when the new InP capacity comes online, and FCF is expected to normalize.

More risk-averse investors are concerned that the company's risks could delay its return to positive FCF, prompting the market to re-rate the stock downward.

Coherent currently has an Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA ratio of 26.56.

Analysts estimate that Coherent will grow its EBITDA at a compound annual growth rate of 30.2%, from $1.64 billion to $2.78 billion over the next two years.

Let us compare these figures with several of its peers. I included this forward EV/EBITDA comparison to present a valuation metric that doesn’t exhibit the same adverse effects as P/E and P/FCF during periods of heavy investment, because EV/EBITDA accounts for Coherent's debt paydown and the operating leverage it gains as new 6-inch wafer fabs ramp up production. The following comparison is an effective means of evaluating companies at different stages of the AI cycle.

In the following comparison, investors generally consider companies undervalued when their forward EV/EBITDA or growth-adjusted forward EV/EBITDA trades below the peer group median.

Company Name Forward EV/EBITDA Two-year EBITDA CAGR Forward EV/EBITDA/growth TTM EBITDA Margin
Applied Optoelectronics 47.51 55.42% 0.86 -6.10
Lumentum 47.35 55.2% 0.86 12.33%
IPG Photonics 37.07 27.35% 1.36 9.62%
Ciena 36.72 22.29% 1.64 9.46%
Fabrinet 29.94 18.2% 1.65 11.27%
Coherent 26.56 30.2% 0.88 19.94%
Viavi Solution 21.20 16.59% 1.28 12.99%
Marvell 19.47 15.39% 1.27 31.39%
Median 36.72 22.29% 1.28 11.27%

Since Coherent has both a forward EV/EBITDA and a Forward EV/EBITDA/growth below the median, investors may consider it undervalued, particularly given that its EBITDA margin is above the median. If the company's strategic plans translate to increased EBITDA growth in line with analysts' expectations, the market likely undervalues Coherent.

However, be aware that this analysis comes with risks. If Coherent is unable to deliver finished InP products, such as transceivers, on the planned timelines, the company may fail to meet analysts' EBITDA growth targets, and investors may begin considering the stock overvalued.

Risks

On the positive side, the CEO said on the second-quarter earnings call, "In terms of wafer starts, we're already at 80% of that goal to double our capacity this [calendar] year." However, the key to doubling capacity by the end of the calendar year is to produce enough 6-inch wafers with a yield higher than that of 3-inch wafers. Coherent has achieved 6-inch wafer yields exceeding those of 3-inch wafers on initial runs at its Sherman, Texas, plant. CEO Anderson stated on the first-quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "I am very pleased to share that our initial 6-inch indium phosphide production yields are actually higher than our current 3-inch indium phosphide yields."

The company has also begun ramping up 6-inch production at a second InP plant in Jarfalla, Sweden, following the success achieved at its first plant. Still, success at one plant doesn't guarantee success at another plant. For instance, if Jarfalla operators fail to calibrate the equipment or environmental controls correctly, or operators at the site lack the expertise to operate the equipment, the yield at its second InP plant might temporarily be lower than expected and slow the overall ramp-up of InP supply. If the Jarfalla plant experiences issues, the market would likely not take the news well.

To complicate matters, Coherent is not just making wafers for one product, but for three: Electro-absorption Modulated Lasers (EML), Continuous-Wave ("CW") lasers, and photodiodes. Each product requires different specifications for the InP wafers (doping type, purity, crystal orientation, and mechanical finish). If the company changes the wafer type it manufactures in reaction to market demand for a specific product and subsequently changes the wafer specifications, it may be unable to sustain the same yield. Another factor is that, as the company transitions from initial small-batch production to full-scale production, it may be unable to preserve the same yield after adjusting various settings. There is some execution risk as Coherent ramps up InP production for various products. Although everything is going well now, there is no guarantee that the company won't encounter obstacles to doubling its InP production.

Another consideration is that the company must turn a wafer into a finished product to generate revenue. CEO Anderson also said on the earnings call, "Typical time from a wafer start to, like, a transceiver shipment is about 6 months." Until now, I have primarily discussed wafer production. However, to get a useful end product like a transceiver, the company must fabricate the desired chips on the wafer, test the chips while on the wafer, dice or cut the wafer into individual chips, retest the chips after dicing, and send them from the Texas or Swedish fab to Malaysia or Vietnam for mounting in a finished package.

A significant risk for the company that may go under the radar for some investors is that, regardless of how successfully the company ramps up wafer production and produces usable chips, if the packaging plants don't mount the chips into packages quickly enough, it could become a bottleneck for shipping transceivers and other end products.

The company's substantial spending to ramp up wafer production at least 6 months ahead of product shipment and revenue generation is the primary reason the company is FCF negative. If the company experiences bottlenecks in packaging and shipping the final product, it could delay its return to FCF-positive status, and the market might revalue the stock downward.

Since the company quadrupled its wafer starts in late 2025, Coherent should begin harvesting the high-margin revenue from those starts in late summer 2026 (the start of FY 2027). If all goes well, the company should begin reversing FCF from negative to positive in FY 2027 as its early investment in 6-inch wafers begins to produce returns. If investors don't observe an improvement in FCF margin by the end of 2027, the stock price may stagnate or decline.

Coherent is a Buy for Long-term Investors

This stock could underperform in calendar year 2026 and possibly even in 2027. Investors pursuing a quick win should avoid Coherent and instead consider Lumentum, which I recently wrote about. Lumentum has excellent prospects of outperforming Coherent over the next year or two.

However, if Coherent returns to positive FCF in FY 2027, the market may be willing to assign it a premium FY 2027 PEG valuation of 1.5—similar to its fiber‑optics peer Ciena, which currently trades at a forward PEG of 1.52. A PEG of 1.5 would imply a stock price of $388.43, a 70% increase from its February 10 closing price of $228.37.

A forward PEG of 1.5 may appear aggressive for a hardware manufacturer. Historically, the market has reserved PEG ratios above 1.5 for Software‑as‑a‑Service (“SaaS”) companies with gross margins above 80%, recurring revenue, and low capital intensity. Many manufacturing companies, by contrast, struggle to sustain PEG ratios above 1.0 due to heavy CapEx, cyclicality, and commodity exposure. But this way of looking at things is evolving. The market is actively re‑rating certain hardware companies that function as “chokepoints” for AI expansion.

AI tailwinds have previously enabled companies such as Vertiv (VRT) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) to trade at forward PEG ratios above 1.5, and Ciena is currently at that level. This demonstrates that the market is willing to revalue manufacturers that furnish essential AI‑enabling components.

Without optical transceivers, AI infrastructure cannot build networks that are fast enough to support next‑generation AI workloads. As a major supplier of components for the fiber‑optic networks underpinning AI, Coherent could be re‑rated higher as it expands operating margins and returns to positive FCF.

It is also possible that Coherent will establish a margin advantage over Lumentum over the next three to five years, given its theoretical structural advantages. In that scenario, Coherent could outperform its rival over the longer term. This bullish case assumes the company avoids the obstacles outlined in the risk section of this article.

I rate Coherent a buy for risk‑tolerant, long‑term, aggressive growth investors willing to wait for the growth story to unfold between 2027 and 2030.

data.title: HF Sinclair: Management Turmoil Creates An Overhang
data.description:
Shares of HF Sinclair (DINO) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 30%. However, investors are disappointed by recent developments, with the stock trading over 10% lower on Wednesday after the company announced Q4 results, with management uncertainty taking center stage. I last covered shares in July, when I rated DINO a “Hold,” and since then, the stock has rallied 16%, ahead of the market’s 10% gain thanks to an improvement in the refining environment, suggesting even a "Buy" was merited. With updated financials and developments on the management front, now is a good time to revisit HF Sinclair.

In the company’s fourth quarter, HF Sinclair earned $1.20 per share, which beat estimates by $0.75. Adjusted EBITDA was $564 million. This was a solid quarter, aided by a favorable regulatory outcome and better refining results than last year as crack spreads normalize. However, attention quickly turned away from results and to other developments.

CEO Leave and Accounting Review Are an Overhang

Before diving into financials, the primary driver of weakness in shares is news that its CEO, Tim Go, is taking a voluntary leave of absence. The current Chair of the Board is stepping in on an interim basis. The Board is also commencing a process to determine if any further interim or permanent actions need to be taken regarding the CEO. The company’s Audit Committee is also assessing the company’s disclosure processes. As a result, the financials released this morning were unaudited. The company expects to release its audited 10-K in a “timely” fashion, but I expect some delay.

Now, situations like this lend themselves to significant speculation, and it is important to not go beyond the facts. To be clear, no one has been accused of wrongdoing, and it is not yet known whether any accounting or disclosure issues are material. This will be for the audit committee and process to determine. In a situation like this, a CEO may propose taking a voluntary absence to pre-empt the Board from taking permanent action. As such, I do not view the fact that his departure is “voluntary” as being either positive or negative; it merely adds to the uncertainty. Irrespective of the outcome, after an announcement like this, whether the CEO can return and retain the trust of the Board and investors is highly uncertain.

As such, there is indisputably a cloud of uncertainty around DINO, and this will be a persistent overhang on shares until the situation is resolved, which will likely take several weeks if not longer. Compared to industries like insurance, refining accounting is more straightforward, especially given my focus on cash flow over GAAP results, so the overhang may not be quite as large. Still, as a general rule, I believe investors need to be very cautious when buying shares of companies undergoing an audit review. As investors, we rely on the accuracy of financial statements and controls to make our judgment on fair value, and incorrect or incomplete releases can skew that judgment.

Refining Macro Lifts Results, but Risks Lie Ahead

Looking at financial results, its refining segment generated $403 million of EBITDA from a $169 million loss last year. Thanks to a better crack spread environment, DINO’s refinery gross margin jumped past $16/barrel from $6.68 a year ago. The company has been a major beneficiary of EPA action to grant small refinery exemptions from the renewable fuel standards after a multiyear backlog of petitions. This lifted EBITDA by $313 million. Still, even excluding this regulatory benefit, EBITDA improved by about $250 million from last year.

That is because the macro refining environment is substantially better. Q4 2024-Q1 2025 represented an especially bad environment as the market struggled with excess supply because refining production was unusually strong following a quiet hurricane season, with crack spreads dropping below $20 (this measures how much refineries make turning crude oil into product). The environment has since stabilized. I view ~$25 as the normalized long-term crack spread, and during Q4 we actually passed $30, though it has since retraced and sits at the $25 level.

Crack spreads are ultimately determined by the relative supply of oil and gasoline/diesel. Going forward, I have some concerns about crack spreads. As you can see below, gasoline inventories became very low during Q4, which is why crack spreads rose. As refiners kept running, that has since caused gasoline inventories to surge even beyond normal levels. Plus, the weaker crude pricing environment may start to limit upstream cap-ex, potentially limiting crude oil supply. One offset is that incremental Venezuelan oil provides U.S. refiners with a cheap feedstock. Still, given the inventory environment, I struggle to see crack spreads rallying much from here. While I still view $25 as the long-term norm, I see more tactical downside risk into the low $20s.

Elsewhere, its marketing segment generated $22 million of EBITDA, up $1 million from last year, thanks to slightly higher volumes, while renewables lost $6 million, a bit better than the $9 million lost last year. I expect renewables to continue to post modest losses. Midstream results continue to run at $114 million, and this should be a fairly stable source of cash flow going forward. Lubricant results were a bit weak, with EBITDA dropping to $43 million from $70 million last year as margins have compressed and sales volumes were weak in a fairly muted environment.

The company continues to maintain a solid balance sheet with $978 million of cash on hand, against which it is carrying $2.8 billion of debt. That leaves it with less than 1x net leverage. Because of its solid balance sheet, HF Sinclair can prioritize capital returns. Shares offer a 3.5% dividend yield, and I view this payout as being highly secure. Beyond this, share buybacks have reduced its share count by 3% over the past year.

While Not Expensive, Caution is Merited in DINO

Back in July, I saw HF Sinclair having ~$1.8 billion of mid-cycle EBITDA and $1 billion of free cash flow capacity. With an easier regulatory backdrop for small refineries, I view DINO as having closer to $1.1 billion of run-rate free cash flow, or about $6/share. This gives shares a nearly ~12% free cash flow yield; DINO is not an expensive stock. However, the whole sector trades at a low multiple. Marathon (MPC) has an 11% capital return yield. Valero (VLO) has a premium multiple, but it still has an over 7% capital return yield. These firms are larger with more advanced refineries, meriting a premium valuation relative to DINO. Plus, they do not have the new overhang of DINO’s CEO leaving.

Particularly given my view that refining spreads may compress in the near term, I see no rush to buy the dip here. This overhang is likely to persist for some time. Part of investing is not only hitting home runs but also avoiding strikeouts. This is why I am structurally cautious on accounting headlines. This may all be resolved, and shares may bounce back, meaning I left some upside on the table. Still, overall, I believe caution is merited. I would rather just own MPC at a double-digit capital return yield and view the risk/reward there much more attractive. As such, I remain a “Hold” and recommend investors remain on the sideline given some risk to crack spreads and DINO-specific uncertainty.

data.title: Brace For Major Moves Ahead: The SCOTUS Ruling On Tariffs Is Imminent
data.description:
The SCOTUS Ruling on Tariffs Is Imminent

The Supreme Court is scheduled to release "opinions" on Feb. 20th, 24th, and 25th. It's very likely that on one of these dates the opinion on the "Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump" case will be announced – that's the case against the Trump's IEEPA-based tariffs.

The tariffs case hearing was expedited; however, the decision is overdue – it was expected in January.

The Likely Decision

The judges were skeptical of the administration's arguments in favor of tariffs at the hearing, and thus, it is very likely that SCOTUS will rule against the tariffs with the statement similar to "President Trump’s broad IEEPA‑based trafficking and reciprocal tariffs exceed the statutory authority granted under the Act."

This ruling would require a rollback of IEEPA tariffs, refunds of already collected tariffs, and essentially establish the limits on "presidential authority over global trade restrictions."

Some judges expressed concerns about the tariff refunds, and thus it's possible that SCOTUS upholds some version of the tariffs, citing "executive foreign‑policy discretion," and thus limits the tariff refunds.

Note, given the delay in decision, some expect that SCOTUS could rule in favor of tariffs and that there is some internal division, with a possible 5th "vote swing." However, in my opinion, SCOTUS did not make the ruling in January because President Trump was at the Davos conference at the time, and the Greenland tariff threat on the EU was active; thus, SCOTUS delayed the decision. The delay does not signal a possible ruling in favor of tariffs.

Likely Immediate Market Reaction

Let's put it this way – the market reaction will be highly uncertain. There are strong arguments in favor of differing market outcomes.

Consensus expectations

These are the consensus expectations for immediate reaction if the SCOTUS rules against the tariffs, including the refunds of tariffs.

10Y yields lower (US10Y) inflationary pressures from the tariffs decrease
Fed cuts more aggressively (US2Y) the risk of goods inflation diminishes
USD weaker (DXY) lower US rates, stronger global economy
S&P 500 higher (SP500) lower rates, Fed cuts 3x, less policy uncertainty
Global stocks higher (EEM) exports increase, positive for the European stocks, emerging markets
Gold down (GLD) geopolitical risk diminishes, with the Sell America trade

Note, the case above would be even stronger if SCOTUS limits the refunds of tariffs. Overall, this would be a major risk-on event.

Alternative scenario

President Trump's economic policy is heavily dependent on IEEPA tariffs.

  • Tariffs are used as the "stick" to encourage more DFI investments into the US, and that's expected to be a major economic driver, increasing construction jobs, manufacturing jobs, and ultimately all jobs. If tariffs are ruled to be illegal, it's likely that the US trade partners would want to renegotiate trade deals and delay or pause the committed DFI investments. Thus, the labor market would continue to weaken, ultimately causing a recession.
  • Tariffs are also used as fiscal revenue to offset fiscal spending and to keep the deficit stable. Without the tariff revenue, the US deficit would increase, potentially resulting in another downgrade of the US debt. The refunds would require an increased supply of Treasuries at the time when global demand for Treasuries is decreasing.
  • Trump could resort to even more drastic measures if the US trading partners demand trade renegotiations; thus, the geopolitical situation could escalate, together with the Sell America trade.

In this situation, the US Treasuries would fall sharply and interest rates would increase. This would counter the consensus expectations of lower 10Y yields, where the focus is more on the likely disinflationary effect, ignoring the geopolitical implications and supply/demand for Treasury bonds.

Thus, if the bond market sells off after the ruling, the stock market reaction is also likely to be negative with a significant selloff – and Gold would spike as a safe haven.

The Key - Trump's Response

The key will be how President Trump will respond if SCOTUS rules against tariffs.

It's widely expected that Trump will reimpose (some) tariffs using different statutes.

  • Section 122 tariffs can reinstate tariffs of up to 15% on any country for 150 days. That gives time for investigations into more consequential permanent tariffs.
  • Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 allows tariffs in response to unfair trade practices by another country, already used for the original China tariffs. No congressional approval is required.
  • Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962. Allows tariffs for national security reasons. Previously used for steel and aluminum tariffs.
  • Section 201 (Safeguard Tariffs) allows temporary tariffs to protect domestic industries from import surges. Requires an investigation by the U.S. International Trade Commission.
  • Section 338 tariffs from 1930s trade wars could be levied on any country seen as discriminating against US commerce. They include tariffs of up to 50% and embargoes on goods deemed a threat.

These are the statutes that Trump can use to reimpose tariffs. However, if the response is "aggressive" other countries are likely to retaliate. In this situation we will have a global trade war escalation – with the Sell America trade, where the US interest rates would rise and the US dollar would weaken.

Then, the question becomes whether a military confrontation would also escalate.

Implications

The financial markets will likely make major moves after SCOTUS makes the ruling on IEEPA tariffs, which is likely imminent.

The benign scenario is where 10Y yields decrease on the news, which triggers a risk-on move. For this scenario, the US response to the decision must be measured, with some tariffs reimposed, but nothing drastic, and without retaliation.

The alternative scenario is if the US response is more drastic with new tariffs and non-tariff policies, which would cause retaliation and global trade war escalation, where the US Treasuries (and the US Dollar) would be vulnerable. This scenario would cause higher interest rates and the risk-off move, with stocks falling and gold rising.

Thus, as the decision is announced, closely monitor the reaction from the 10Y Treasury Bonds. Will update as the decision is announced.

data.title: Not A Bear Market Yet, But It's Already A Stock Picker's Dream
data.description:
I believe that lately the market has been more euphoric than usual, both in terms of optimism and pessimism, and as a stock picker, I find this moment very interesting. It is debatable whether or not we are in a bear market. I mean, sentiment is not the best, but at the same time, the indexes have not fallen to the point where we can say, “We definitely are in a bear market.”

For instance, the S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV) and Nasdaq (QQQ) are only less than 6% below their all-time highs (I will refer to these as ATHs from now on), and to be considered a bear market, we need to see corrections of ~-20% (at least).

But at the same time, if you, like me, are a stock picker, you may already be feeling that valuations are becoming more attractive, which is why I think that currently, even though we are not really seeing a generalized bear market, there are already good opportunities to the point where we can say that for stock pickers, we are already in a dip.

Many Important Companies Have Fallen Sharply

One of the most emblematic examples is Microsoft (MSFT), which, although still one of the leading companies in terms of market cap, is already almost 30% below its ATH. And when we try to find the reason, it is even difficult to be sure that the market punished MSFT stock for x reasons.

For instance, a good part of MSFT's decline occurred after the last earnings release, and the company exceeded estimates, delivering Azure growth in line with market expectations. However, apparently, many analysts wanted to see even greater growth in the Cloud division than what was delivered. Other than that, to maintain this growth, CapEx is very high, not to mention the nuances regarding guidance. But anyway, it's not as if Microsoft has ceased to be Microsoft or that its long-term prospects have changed; it was more a matter of market sentiment, rotation, uncertainty, and, of course, valuation correction.

Amazon (AMZN) is another good example. It is already trading at ~-23% of its ATH, and the case has hardly changed. The reasons for this sell-off are also closely related to cloud, CapEx, and other uncertainties regarding tech. Okay, CapEx was indeed a significant surprise and will change the free cash flow for 2026 by billions, but should that affect the market so much? I don't know; in my view, it's also a bit of an overreaction.

Netflix (NFLX) also falls into this category. The company continues to grow and post solid numbers. The investment case that this is one of the best entertainment companies right now, able to create highly engaging content and improve its financials, remains intact, obviously with a little more uncertainty after the news related to Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). But note that we are not talking about a residual decline this time, but rather a decline of more than 40% from the ATH. This caused me to change my rating for NFLX from hold to buy (check it here) because the margin of safety has improved significantly.

Anyway, if I were to cite all the examples, this article would never end, but the chart below shows that most large companies, even Apple (AAPL), which is not very involved in these trends (such as high CapEx), have already corrected since their ATH. In fact, it is also worth mentioning that others that are not mega caps have also been hit and have already fallen sharply, such as Uber (UBER) and Ferrari N.V. (RACE).

So, Why Didn't The Indexes Fall?

Well, obviously, if the big tech companies in the sector fell sharply, but indexes such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq fell less, something held back their performance. And the highlights here are quite clear: basically, basic materials and energy performed well above average. We can even mention Utilities, which also did well over a 6-month period, but the total return performance of basic materials and energy was much higher.

Note that even if we look at the year-to-date (which is barely two months old), the Basic Materials sector (IYM) has already posted a total return of ~19%, while Energy (XLE) is already up more than 21%, and Utilities (XLU) has also held up well, with a 9% total return. In addition, it is worth mentioning that these three are not far from their ATH, while XLK, which is the technology ETF, has already accumulated a decline of ~9%.

Part of this is also related to precious metals, especially gold and silver, which performed very well during the period and pulled up stocks such as Newmont (NEM), which rose 159% in the last year (+24% YTD), and AngloGold (AU), which rose 220% in one year (+25% YTD). Both are very important holdings in the IYM.

There are two points to justify that we are not in a bear market. Some trends, such as the increase in commodities, held up the main indexes, such as the “euphoria” with gold, silver, copper, and the like, but also because there was a certain rotation. It is not a generalized decline in stocks; investors are still concerned about currency, inflation, geopolitics, and the macro landscape, but they are also wary of tech, so we saw this rotation to other types of stocks, such as utilities, which also benefited.

Why This Is Good For Stock Pickers

All of this corroborates my thesis that we do not need to buy “indexes.” My portfolio is not and does not need to be a proxy for the market. As I am a stock picker and follow sectors such as tech more closely, I have been buying more recently, precisely because prices are better and I can be more confident. Amazon is one of the companies I wanted to increase my position in, but I already had “enough.” However, at the current price, I see much more margin of safety, so I allow myself to have a slightly higher position.

And based on Amazon's valuation, I would like to make a provocative statement.

While Amazon is falling due to uncertainties, Walmart (WMT) is seen as a much more solid case and continues to rise, with a forward P/E of 43x, a 70% premium. Even if you believe that Amazon's current CapEx puts pressure on Free Cash Flow and you don't know if it will really have the return Amazon expects, don't you think this premium is a little too much? After all, we are talking about Walmart, which has reliable but gradual growth prospects, versus Amazon, which is still growing rapidly and, with massive investments, may grow even more.

I don't think Walmart is a bad company; in fact, I would have it in my portfolio at the “right” price. But what I mean is that, at the moment, I'm not even considering Walmart. I'm buying Amazon, and if in five years Walmart is dirt cheap while Amazon is overvalued, I'll consider rotating.

Another stock I bought again was Uber. As much as I agree that it is a company with a slightly more uncertain future, we are talking about a company trading at a low P/E ratio and still growing EPS at a very fast pace. And since I think the market's fear of disruption is much greater than necessary, I think it's an asymmetric stock.

These are just a few examples; you don't have to do the same. My point here is that I'm basically buying things against the market trend, and that's interesting. As I mentioned in my controversial article on Barrick (B) (you can check it out here), I don't want to be a contrarian just for the sake of being a contrarian, but with these market movements that I consider to be euphoric both upwards and downwards, I prefer to filter out the good opportunities that have already fallen too far and pass on the “opportunities” that are already closer to fair valuation or even overvalued.

This also ends up giving me a certain degree of decorrelation with the S&P 500 and even with the stocks I own. I don't buy Amazon every month, and now that the price is better, I end up adding more shares than when the price is worse. This means that when AMZN stock performs at ~10% for the year, I can often outperform that, since throughout the year, I only bought where I believed the margin of safety was wide enough. Obviously, the intention here is not to catch the dip, and perhaps Amazon will continue to correct, which could take away some of the effect I mentioned, but for me, buying good companies at good prices is enough.

Another point that this moment of “bear market, but for stock pickers” reveals to me is the importance of having cash. At times when you don't find much to buy or don't have much conviction, the ideal is to reduce your aggressiveness a little and accumulate cash to buy the dips. This also goes against the contrarian movement I mentioned. It's not contrarian for the sake of being contrarians, but when Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) was not the market consensus, that was when I bought the most GOOGL shares, and now that the stock has risen, even though I remain optimistic, it's a time when I reduce that appetite a little.

Bottom Line

We, as individual investors, don't need to beat the benchmark in the next six months, and that's a very important edge.

This means we don't have to invest according to momentum, and we can stick to the long term. If gold really continues to rise sharply in the short term, okay, I just missed out on a gain, but I also didn't risk trying to ride a short-term trend.

On the other hand, I am quite satisfied buying companies that I am highly confident will be able to compound their fundamentals in the coming years, and all this at a much better price than I saw a few months ago. Therefore, I believe that the current momentum is interesting for investing, but that it is necessary to exercise a certain amount of caution and, above all, to have a good filter to distinguish between market overreaction and real opportunity.

data.title: AMD Stock: $300 Appears Imminent; Why I'm Buying More
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares have been volatile the last few months as sentiment has waxed and waned with the AI sector at large, which has experienced some scares over that timeframe. With AI capex accelerating in 2026, I anticipate AMD will have a transformative year.

In my last piece on AMD, I discussed the company's partnership with OpenAI and how its upcoming lineup of GPUs would generate upward momentum for the stock. I rated it a Strong Buy, and it has since declined around 14% relative to a 2% gain for the S&P 500 (SPY). That article can be read here.

I've been riding AMD through some major peaks and valleys since first rating it a Buy back in 2015 when it was $2 per share (article here), so I'm not too concerned about the current holding pattern. In fact, while the market appears to be souring on the company's role in the next chapter of the AI story, I think now is the perfect time to bolster an existing position or to open an inaugural one. And the former is precisely what I'm doing.

Let's quickly recap AMD's Q4 2025 earnings results:

As expected, the company's shift to the data center and increased demand for its GPUs propelled revenue to $10.3 billion, another all-time high, up 34% YoY and 11% QoQ. EPS ballooned to $1.53, up 40% YoY and 28% QoQ as both operating margin (+20 bps) and gross margin (+30 bps) improved.

For those who follow me and read my articles often, you'll know that my two favorite words in stock analysis are "operating leverage" -- companies that can boost margins while generating higher sales are a major green flag, and AMD is doing that in spades and has been for a few years now:

This has been led by success in the data center and also in the client domain with its Ryzen line of CPUs, which have become best-in-class as Intel (INTC) is still struggling to find its footing amid ongoing manufacturing woes. Meanwhile, TSMC (TSM) is having no such woes and has vaulted AMD to the top in x86 server and consumer CPUs.

With this titan at its back, the company has been blowing away YoY comps despite a solid showing in the previous 12 months:

Data Center revenue, which includes EPYC servers CPUs and Instinct GPUs, rose 39% YoY and 24% QoQ; Client and Gaming rose 37% YoY and fell 3% QoQ; and even Embedded had a solid showing, up 3% YoY and 11% QoQ. But most importantly, all of these revenue increases were accompanied by even larger increases in profits.

AMD's favorable balance sheet remains pristine as cash & equivalents & short-term investments grew to $10.5 billion relative to just $2.3 billion in long-term debt, demonstrating the upsides of a fab-less chip business. Free cash flow also came in at $2.1 billion for the quarter, a record.

On the outlook side, AMD provided the following guidance:

The revenue midpoint here of $9.8 billion would be 32% YoY growth and a 5% sequential decline, and EPS is expected to be around $1.25 at the midpoint. Gross margin of 55% indicates a slight decline QoQ but is still above the final 2024 and 2025 annual figures.

In the wake of this report and guidance, shares plummeted by nearly 20% and are still floating around those levels. While the results seem solid (Q1 is typically a soft quarter) and beat consensus estimates, earnings were announced while the AI sector was having a mini-meltdown, which didn't help sentiment, and there seem to be concerns about revenue from China in 2026.

Whatever the reason, I think investors would do well to ignore the noise and focus on the big picture for this year: AI capex.

The hyperscalers have all committed to absolutely massive increases in capex spending on AI data center build-outs as they all race to be the leader in the most expensive arms race in history. Many are wondering whether these projects will actually generate commensurate ROI, myself included, but that's almost irrelevant to the AMDs of the world -- if these companies want to spend hundreds of billions of dollars to meet AI demand, that's a boon to the supply chain even if that value proposition doesn't shake out years down the line.

So yes, demand and ROI are under scrutiny, but the capex is real regardless. AMD, as a supplier for the CPUs and GPUs that make these data centers tick, stands to benefit massively. The company's MI400 series remains on track to launch in 2H2026 as does its Helios rack-scale platform, which has been largely enabled by the acquisition of ZT Systems and allows the company to challenge Nvidia's (NVDA) rack-scale dominance. I anticipate this new generation of processors will expand AMD's current standing in the inferencing market and will woo some big-name customers as the release date approaches.

Risks

There are some risks here that I should acknowledge. First, the RAMpocalypse shows no signs of slowing down anytime soon, which will jack up the costs of GPUs and could dampen demand. This might also be felt in Ryzen sales as high memory prices suppress overall PC appetite. I'm not too concerned about this factor since I think the hyperscalers will be willing to pay higher prices, which will offset lower demand on the Client side, but investors should still keep an eye on how that mix affects overall revenue and margins.

Second, I've written a fair bit about the ASIC era and how it will hurt pricing power in the AI accelerator market (see my bear article on how this will affect Nvidia (NVDA) here), so wouldn't that affect AMD too? It could be the reason I think Nvidia will feel the pain more from this development is due to the company's lofty valuation and monopolistic margins -- losing pricing power hurts the market leader that's priced to maintain dominance more than it hurts the small fish that's priced like, well, a small fish.

Investor Takeaway

Despite the pullback, I think AMD is in prime position for 2026 as it continues its dominance in the server CPU space and prepares for the launch of the MI400 series of Instinct GPUs. As these design wins come through and the record AI capex from hyperscalers flows into the supply chain, we will likely see AMD achieve record earnings for the year.

In the short term, the stock will likely remain volatile, especially as rumors of MI400 delays spread throughout the market, but in the medium term (12 months) and long term, I continue to see AMD as a winner.

Shares are currently trading at a forward PEG ratio of 0.63, which is well below its peers like Nvidia at 1.04 and Broadcom (AVGO) at 1.02. As the company's data center prospects play out in the quarters ahead, driven by higher AI capex throughout the industry, I think we'll see these ratios converge, sending AMD up near and even beyond the $300 range.

On the back of these catalysts, I am reiterating a Strong Buy on AMD and will be accumulating shares at these levels in anticipation of a significant move higher in the months ahead.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: GOF: Why Discounts (Premiums) Matter When Buying CEFs (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When evaluating closed-end funds ("CEFs") for potential income investments, it is often wise to pay attention to the current price relative to the NAV (net asset value) of the fund, which is described as the Discount if market price is below NAV or Premium if the Price is higher than the current NAV. Because the market price is highly sensitive to investor sentiment regarding the fund, the asset class represented by fund holdings, the current macro environment, and even the current fear vs greed measure relative to CEFs in general, the discount or premium can often fluctuate, sometimes wildly. Therefore, knowing when to buy a CEF can be advantageous since we can only buy at market price, and the best time to buy is typically when the fund trades at a wider-than-average discount (or a lower-than-average premium).

In my recent review of Nuveen Real Asset Income and Growth Fund (JRI), I went into even more detail regarding how to evaluate a CEF and when is the best time to buy one if you are considering it. For income-oriented investors like me, CEFs can provide high-yield current income while often delivering strong total returns over time, although performance can fluctuate from year to year based on the macro environment at the time. That is the case with Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF), which I last rated Hold in May 2024 as the premium had run up again to near 30% above NAV.

Now, the relative premium has dropped considerably over the past couple of months, and GOF is in Buy territory again. We have seen this pattern repeat over the past few years, with GOF typically trading at a very high premium most of the time. Only twice in the past three years has the premium dropped to around 5%, first in October 2023 and then again in December 2025. The pricing chart on CEFConnect shows this pattern quite clearly.

I am a buyer of GOF at a premium below 10%. As the fund approaches its 20-year anniversary (with an inception date of July 2007), it still boasts a current yield of 17.7% and is one of only a handful of CEFs that have been around for more than ten years and have never cut the distribution.

When I first wrote about GOF in May 2023, I suggested that new investors should wait for a “strategic opportunity” to invest in the fund, suggesting that the premium was too high at the time. That opportunity happened in October 2023 and was a good time to acquire new shares of the fund.

With much of the credit sector getting hammered in the opening months of 2026, including BDCs (BIZD), CLOs like OXLC, and many fixed income funds that are exposed to private credit such as FS Specialty Lending Fund (FSSL) and FS Credit Opportunities (FSCO) getting clobbered over fears of AI disruption, GOF offers a potential opportunity due to the fear driving down the market price.

In fact, according to CEFData.com, the one-year total return for GOF at NAV was nearly 16%; however, at market price, the fund only delivered a total return of -7.44%!

We can see how the NAV declined slightly over the past year as the fund distributed more than it earned; however, the negative total return in that time was entirely due to the market price dropping from a premium above 35% to as low as 5% before climbing back up to about 9% today, as shown on CEFConnect.

Despite the recent poor price performance of GOF, the average annual returns since inception (as of 12/31/25) still exceeded 10% annually based on both market price and NAV as shown on the fund website.

That is outstanding performance for any fund and something we might expect to see from an equity fund with over 10% annual returns for 18 years. However, GOF is a fixed income fund that delivers strong total returns with mostly high-yield income and some (but not much) capital appreciation.

The stated investment objective of GOF is to maximize total return by a combination of current income and capital appreciation. Over the past 18 years, the fund has accomplished that by trading at a premium to NAV for most of the fund’s history, and especially over the past ten years, as shown on the fund fact sheet.

This chart pattern suggests that the strategy of buying GOF when it trades at a very low premium, or even better, buying when it trades at a discount like it did briefly during the Covid pandemic in 2020, would result in the highest total return. Until that happens, buying at a relatively low premium is the next best option for maximizing total return.

Because this is an update to my previous coverage of GOF, I do not intend to get into too much detail regarding the fund, but a quick overview and some basic information about this fixed income CEF are provided in the section below.

Fund Overview and Key Facts

GOF is a CEF that falls into the category of multi-sector taxable bond fund, according to both CEFConnect and CEFData, which are both useful websites for evaluating CEFs. The sector allocation from the fund fact sheet shows the highest allocation to bank loans at nearly 37% of total portfolio holdings, followed by high-yield corporate bonds at 27.6% (as of the end of 2025).

The fund has about $2.5B in total assets and employs modest leverage of about 12%. With more than 1,400 total holdings in the fund portfolio, those holdings are well diversified with no single position accounting for more than 1% of the total.

Credit quality of the fund holdings, as shown on the fund website, is about half investment grade (BB-rated or higher) and half junk-rated (“junk” implies below investment grade, B-rated, or lower).

Lower credit rating indicates a higher risk, and those lower-rated or junk bonds, as they are colloquially known, pay higher interest rates, helping to enable the high-yield distribution that GOF offers. At the current market price and distribution rate, GOF offers a very enticing distribution yield of nearly 18%.

GOF Distributions

One advantage to holding a fund like GOF in an Income Compounder portfolio like mine, where the majority of distributions are reinvested into additional shares of high-yielding income funds, is the compounding effect that leads to a snowballing income stream. Because a large component of the total return comes from the income that the fund distributes, by reinvesting into more shares each month, you can create a growing income stream over time. This strategy is further enabled by the fund’s DRIP (dividend reinvestment program) policy of issuing new shares at a discount to market price of up to 5% when the fund trades at a premium.

For listed closed-end funds, the reinvestment price is determined by one of two scenarios. One, if the common shares are trading at a discount, the DRIP price is the weighted average cost to purchase the common shares from the NYSE or elsewhere. Lastly, if the common shares are trading at a premium, the DRIP price is then determined by the higher of the NAV per common share or approximately 95% of the common share price.

GOF pays a steady, monthly, managed distribution and has never reduced that distribution since its inception in 2007, not even after the Great Recession of 2008-9, nor after the Covid pandemic in 2020, nor the bear market of 2022. It last increased the monthly distribution in May 2013 to $0.1821, where it has remained since.

Of course, the fund relies on ROC in some years to maintain that high-yield distribution, and that can be one attractive aspect of the fund when held in a taxable account. According to the fund website, the fund distributions consisted of 67% ROC in the 2025 tax year.

If you are unfamiliar with the concept of ROC, you may assume that it’s a bad thing and suggest that the distribution cannot be maintained at such a high yield forever. However, that is not the case, as history suggests. The basics of Return of Capital are explained in detail by my colleague, Steven Bavaria, here.

Risks to Buy Thesis

Funds like GOF that offer a managed distribution can often maintain a much higher yielding payout than the fund earns by relying on ROC in some years; however, it cannot maintain that distribution over time if the ROC is “destructive” to NAV. In recent months, the amount of ROC included in the distribution may have been reduced slightly, at least according to CEFConnect, which shows that some short-term capital gains have been included in the last several months distributions.

Perhaps this is an indication that a shift away from ROC in 2026 is occurring. However, the most recent 19a notice from January 2026 would suggest that the information presented by CEFConnect is inaccurate. It states that 70% of the January distribution consists of ROC and the other 30% is income.

We are pleased to report the payment of the Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund’s (the “Fund”) monthly distribution in the amount of $0.1821 per share payable on January 30, 2026 to shareholders of record on January 15, 2026. The Fund currently anticipates that $0.05433 per share, or 29.84% of this distribution would currently be classified as income and $0.12777 per share, or 70.16% of this distribution would currently be classified as return of capital. A final determination of the tax character of distributions paid by the Fund in 2026 will be reported to shareholders in January 2027 on Form 1099DIV.

If the distribution is not fully covered in 2026, there is a risk that the distribution could be cut in the future. The history of the fund suggests that such a cut, if it were to happen, would not likely be prior to the end of 2026 unless the fund changes its “managed distribution” policy. And given the history of the fund through all kinds of market cycles since inception, I believe that such a cut is unlikely.

Summary: Buy GOF While the Premium is Low

For income investors who wish to maximize total returns from their holdings in high-yield CEFs, the best time to buy or add shares is when the discount (or premium) is well below the recent average, especially if that lower price is due to market “fear” rather than fundamental reasons.

In my opinion, GOF offers such a buying opportunity at a price premium below 10%.

As I compose this article, GOF trades at a premium of about 9% and offers a steady, monthly 18% yield along with a discount to reinvest. For long-term income compounders like me, now is a good time to Buy GOF, in my opinion. I own shares of GOF in my personal Income Compounder portfolio as part of a diversified set of passive income holdings that helps to spread the risk among asset classes.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alphabet: Gemini And Google Cloud Put Company In The AI Revolution's Pantheon
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ: (GOOGL)), in November 2025, I analyzed the road ahead for investors post the company’s Q3 report, especially around the time that the AI trade was losing some steam heading into the end of 2025. I had a Buy rating on the name.

Since the article was published, the stock has gained 3.55%, comfortably outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which has declined 0.36% during the same period.

GOOGL reported its Q4 numbers about 2 weeks back, and while the numbers were great, investors were still spooked by the blockbuster capex spending that the company announced. In the last one month, the stock has lost about 6.54%, primarily driven by the post-earnings selloff.

In this article, I analyze the company’s progress made towards its AI monetization efforts and also evaluate whether the company’s investments to date are yielding positive results or whether investors are right to be rattled by the company’s plans to pump more money than before into its AI efforts.

A Snapshot of Third Quarter Results

Alphabet finished the year strongly in my view, with Q4 performance comfortably beating both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $113.82 billion, up 18% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $2.34 billion. Diluted EPS came in at $2.82, up 31% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.18. Operating margins held steady at 32%, an improvement of 150 bps [or 1.5%] sequentially. The margins were impacted by a $2.1 billion stock-based compensation charge related to Waymo. Free cash flows came in at $24.6 billion, allowing the company to end the year with $126.8 billion in cash and marketable securities.

With respect to guidance, the biggest surprise was the announcement related to capital expenditures. More specifically, management announced that 2026 capex would be in the range between $175 billion and $185 billion, which translates to investments nearly doubling compared to 2025. The ramp-up of investments was attributed to continued hiring in AI and cloud infrastructure. Management also announced that depreciation for 2026 is expected to “meaningfully increase for the full year.”

Search Business Sets Another Record as AI Tools Act as Agents of Growth and Not Agents of Cannibalization

In my previous analysis on GOOGL, I mentioned how the company’s search business is continuing to show strong growth even in an increasingly competitive landscape driven by AI. The good news for GOOGL investors is that Q3 was no fluke. On the contrary, it could very well have been an inflection point. This is because Q4 saw the search business set another record, blowing past the growth record it set in Q3. More specifically, revenue from search came in at $63.1 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 16.7%, its highest growth rate ever. Even on a sequential basis, the growth of 11.5% was very strong. The last time GOOGL set a double-digit sequential growth in Search was back in Q4 of FY21, which demonstrates the strength of the growth seen in the segment.

During the earnings call, CEO Sundar Pichai commented the following on the search business: “Search saw more usage than ever before, with AI continuing to drive an expansionary moment.” The statement to me is very telling since it supports the notion that the company’s AI tools, such as AI Overviews and AI Models, are not acting as agents of cannibalization but are instead strong growth catalysts for the search business.
